edge: The task of inferring a meaningful geometric scene representation from a single image is highlighted as a fundamental problem in computer vision.
Traditional depth map prediction approaches mentioned can only reason about areas that are visible in the image.
Neural radiance fields are described as capable of capturing true 3D representations including color, but are mentioned as too complex to be generated from a single image.
The proposed approach involves predicting an implicit density field from a single image, mapping every location in the frustum of the image to volumetric density.
The scene representation complexity is reduced by directly sampling color from available views instead of storing it in the density field.
A neural network is mentioned as capable of predicting the scene representation in a single forward pass.
The training of the network is mentioned to be through self-supervision from only video data.

node_2: depth prediction and novel view synthesis
edge: The formulation allows volume rendering to perform both depth prediction and novel view synthesis.
Inferring information about the geometric structure of a scene from a single image is highlighted as important for applications such as robotics and augmented reality.
Computer vision and deep learning are key concepts in the context, where traditional computer vision has evolved into deep learning age.
Inferring a 3D scene from a single image is a prominent challenge highlighted in the context, emphasizing the shift towards single-image depth prediction.
Depth maps are typically used to represent per-pixel depth values in computer vision tasks, showcasing how the problem has been traditionally formulated.
Neural networks have shown the capability of accurate single-image depth prediction, indicating the advancements in self-supervised training using video sequences.
Depth prediction methods focus on modeling single depth values per pixel, limiting the ability to capture the true 3D representation of the scene.
In novel view synthesis, the goal is to infer a representation allowing rendering views from novel viewpoints, rather than directly reasoning about the scene geometry.
Neural radiance field based methods achieve impressive results but require many training images per scene and do not generalize to new scenes.
Efforts have been made to condition the neural network on global or local scene features to enable generalization.
Conditioning the neural network on global or local scene features has only been shown to work well on simple scenes.
Obtaining a neural radiance field from a single image has not been achieved before.
The problem tackled in this work is inferring a geometric representation from a single image.
The depth prediction formulation is generalized to a continuous density field in this work.
The architecture in this work contains an encoder-decoder network that predicts a dense feature map from the input image.
The feature map predicted from the input image locally conditions a density field inside the camera frustum in our method.
The density field inside the camera frustum can be evaluated at any spatial point through a multi-layer perceptron (MLP).
The multi-layer perceptron (MLP) is fed with the coordinates of the point and the feature sampled from the predicted feature map.
To train the method, simple image reconstruction losses are relied upon.
The method in this work achieves robust generalization and accuracy.

Color sampling is a key novelty in achieving robust generalization and accurate geometry prediction. It involves sampling color values directly from input frames through reprojection instead of using MLP to predict color values.
Accurate geometry prediction is achieved through image reconstruction losses by forcing the model to adhere to multi-view consistency assumption during training.
Color sampling is applied when performing volume rendering to sample color values directly from input frames through reprojection.
Volume rendering is essential for accurate geometry prediction as mentioned in the context.
The capacity is shifted to the feature extractor to reduce the complexity of MLP. The encoder-decoder captures the entire scene in the extracted features, and MLP evaluates those features locally.
To enable robust training, the capacity of MLP is significantly reduced, and a more powerful encoder-decoder is used to capture the entire scene in the extracted features.

The continuous nature of density fields allows reconstruction of a novel view from the colors of any frame by applying a reconstruction loss between two frames that observe areas occluded in the input frame.

A neural network architecture predicts the geometric structure of a scene from a single image.
A scene is represented as a continuous density field in the context.
The architecture learns geometry, even in occluded areas.
A point in world coordinates can be projected onto the image plane.
The geometric structure of a scene is represented by a function mapping scene coordinates to volume density, known as a density field.
An encoder-decoder network predicts a pixel-aligned feature map from the input image to represent the distribution of local geometry.
Each feature at a pixel location captures the distribution of local geometry along the ray from the camera origin through that pixel.
The density field is designed to lie inside the camera frustum.
For points outside the frustum, features are extrapolated from within the frustum.
To obtain a density value at a 3D coordinate, a process is described in the context.
To obtain a density value at a 3D coordinate x, we first project x onto the input image u′ I = πI(x) and bilinearly sample the feature fu′ = F(u′) at that position.

This feature fu′, along with the positional encoding [32] γ(d) of the distance d between x and the camera origin, and the positional encoding γ(u′ I) of the pixel, is then passed to a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) ϕ.

During training, ϕ and F learn to describe the density of the scene given the input view. We can interpret the feature representation fu′ as a descriptor of the density along a ray through the camera center and pixel u′.
In turn, ϕ acts as a decoder, that given fu′ and a distance to the camera, predicts the density at the 3D location x.
σx = ϕ(fu′ I, γ(d), γ(u′ I))(1)
Unlike most current works on neural fields, we do not use ϕ to also predict color.
This drastically reduces the complexity of the distribution along a ray as density distributions tend to be simple, while color often contains complex high-frequency components.
When rendering the scene from a novel viewpoint, we do not retrieve color from our scene representation directly. Instead, we sample the color for a point in 3D space from the available images.
